title: The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles
subtitle: The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.
caption:
The gender wage ratio in different income percentiles, United States, 1980 to 2010

The gender ratio refers to the raw female-male wage ratio. "Ninetieth percentile" indicates an income higher than that of 90% of the population - thus, for example, in 2010 a 74% ratio in the ninetieth percentile indicates that the highest earning women made 74% of the hourly wage of the highest earning men.

Across three decades in the United States, the raw female-male wage ratio shows distinct trajectories for workers at different income levels. The vertical axis measures the ratio from 0% to 100%, while the horizontal axis spans from 1980 to 2010. Three lines track the fiftieth, tenth, and ninetieth percentiles. In 1980, the tenth percentile line begins around 65%, the ninetieth percentile near 62%, and the fiftieth percentile at 60%. By 1990, the tenth percentile rises to approximately 75%, the ninetieth percentile reaches 76%, and the fiftieth percentile climbs to 73%. The fiftieth percentile line continues upward through 2010, ending near 82%, the highest value among all three series at the chart's conclusion. The tenth percentile line follows a similar upward path, finishing just below 81% in 2010. The ninetieth percentile line shows a different pattern: after rising to 76% in 1990, it plateaus and remains flat through 2010 at approximately 74%. The gap between the fiftieth and ninetieth percentiles narrows over time, with the middle-income workers showing the most consistent improvement in their wage ratio relative to men. The data shows that workers in the middle and lower income ranges experienced larger gains in gender wage parity than those at the highest income levels during this period.
